result:
The first time I realized that time might be a place, I was standing in the checkout line of a grocery store that no longer exists. It was a Kroger in a shrinking Ohio town, the kind where the fluorescent lights hum a little too loud and the linoleum has the color of old teeth. I was eight, maybe nine, holding a melting popsicle box against my chest like contraband. The woman in front of me wore a polyester dress printed with tiny windmills. She smelled of vinegar and dime-store perfume, and when she turned—just for a second—I saw my own face reflected in her glasses. Not a child’s face, but the one I wear now, older, stubbled, startled. She smiled as if we shared a secret, then paid for her saltines and left. By the time I blinked, the parking lot outside was empty except for a single seagull standing on a cart coral, screaming at nothing.

I never told anyone. Kids know the penalty for sounding crazy: extra chores, a pediatrician with cold stethoscope fingers, the gentle erosion of credibility that begins with “I saw…” and ends with Santa Claus dismantled. So I filed the moment away like a postcard from a country I hadn’t visited yet. But the idea lodged: if I could meet myself in a stranger’s lens, then maybe every second is a room we keep walking through without noticing the doors.

Years later, long after that Kroger was razed and replaced by a dialysis center, I became a collector of obsolete maps. Not antiques—just the Rand McNally road atlases my parents used before GPS. I’d buy them for a quarter at yard sales, the covers sun-bleached, the spine cracked like an old choir hymnal. Inside, the continent lay flattened, veins of red and blue highways promising arrival. But the real attraction was the margins, where previous owners had left hieroglyphs of their lives: a circle around Gatlinburg, a penciled “Motel 6—bedbugs!!”, a phone number with a name that wasn’t in the book anymore. Each atlas was a palimpsest of intentions, detours, and regrets. I liked to imagine that if I drove the exact route someone else had plotted in 1987, I might intercept the echo of their windshield, catch a fragment of argument or lullaby still ricocheting between the guardrails.

I tested the theory on a Tuesday in March. My life at the time resembled a loading screen—relationship stalled, job reduced to a series of Zoom squares in which my chin looked surprised to be attached to the rest of me. So I took the 1993 atlas, opened to page 37, and followed a purple Sharpis h trail that began in Indianapolis, dipped south to Owensboro, then zig-zagged east through Daniel Boone National Forest. The notation simply said “Watermelon.” No context, no date. I drove until dusk, past pawn shops advertising guns and guitars, past barns collapsing like old men sighing into their hands. Just outside Slade, Kentucky, I found a fruit stand shuttered for two decades, plywood windows tattooed with faded concert posters. But there, in the gravel, lay a single rotting rind, blackened and half-buried, as if the earth itself had tried to digest it and given up. I squatted, touched the rind’s leathery curve, and felt the same vertigo as in the grocery line: time folding, origami style, the past refusing to be polite and stay behind.

I began to schedule these pilgrimages monthly. A dashed line toward “Uncle Ray’s ashes?” took me to a boat ramp on the Mississippi where an old man was selling catfish out of a cooler. He insisted Ray had been his bass-fishing buddy until a thunderstorm turned the river into a butcher’s block. “Lightning don’t baptize,” he said, packing my purchase in newspaper. “It just renames.” Another annotation—“First kiss, stupid hat”—steered me to a drive-in theater converted into a swap meet. I bought a Cubs cap from a woman who claimed her late husband proposed to her during a double feature of Jaws and The Apple Dumpling Gang. She handed me the hat free of charge. “Take it,” she said. “Memory shouldn’t collect interest.”

Each trip rearranged me a little. I began to notice how highways smell different at every longitude: chlorine sweetness near chemical plants, wet pennies near foundries, the cabbage breath of farmland after rain. Radio signals carried zombie broadcasts—preachers, truckers, polka shows—voices unaware their era had ended. I started recording them on a handheld cassette deck, labeling the cassettes with the same cryptic minimalism I’d inherited: “Gas station, 2 a.m., someone laughing.” Sometimes I played them back at home, letting the warble of outdated magnetic tape fill my apartment like incense. The laughter on one felt contagious until I realized it was punctuated by sobbing; the two emotions braided so tightly they could be the same vine.

One night, drunk on diner coffee and the loneliness that passes for freedom, I annotated my own atlas. I drew a star where I-64 crosses the Wabash, wrote “Here I split open.” No metaphor; I’d actually dropped a pocketknife while changing a tire, sliced my palm, and bled onto the asphalt before I could curse. The blood looked black under the sodium lights, like oil that had learned to be human. I pressed my hand to the page, a greasy print, then drove on. Months later, returning to that same bridge, I found a fresh exit ramp, concrete still pale. Construction crews had shifted the geography, moved the wound. My handprint—if it ever lingered—was ground into aggregate, reborn as someone else’s foundation. I felt a strange lightness: the map had become a conversation, my scar translated into infrastructure.

People ask what I’m looking for. I tell them I’m not looking, I’m listening. Every mile is a syllable in a sentence spoken by strangers who will never meet. The complete paragraph would break your heart with its casual majesty: mile 212, child learns to tie shoes; mile 213, woman decides against suicide because the sky looks like her grandmother’s quilt; mile 214, trucker spots a silver balloon caught on a speed limit sign and reads his daughter’s name in its wrinkled skin. None of these events care whether we believe in them; they accumulate like lint in the dryer of history,悄无声息 yet flammable.

The last atlas I bought had belonged to a traveling nurse. Her route spiraled inward, from Portland, Oregon to a small coastal town the map indexed but could not pronounce. On the final page she wrote, in ballpoint so furious it tore the paper, “The ocean is the only clock that tells the truth.” I drove there in October, parked on basalt cliffs buffeted by wind that tasted of shipwrecks and iodine. The sun was a fraudulent coin slipping between clouds. I opened the driver’s door, stood on the running board, and for the first time in years felt the grocery-store vertigo return—not a memory but an invitation. The horizon stitched sky to water with a silver thread. Somewhere behind me, engines coughed, gulls laughed, my phone buzzed with deadlines. Ahead, nothing but the long sentence continuing, word after word, wave after wave.

I haven’t annotated that page. Some maps you enter instead of labeling. Maybe that’s what the nurse meant: the clock that tells the truth isn’t keeping time, it’s keeping place. And we are never late; we are always here, again and again, until the door we didn’t notice opens, and we walk through, carrying our melting popsicles, our obsolete atlases, our stupid hats, into the next room that was always this one, waiting patiently for us to arrive.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
